questionText: The random variable $Z$ has the standard normal distribution, $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Find $P(Z < 1.2)$, giving your answer to $4$ significant figures.

steps.explanation: The standard normal variable has mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$. Its cumulative area to the left of a value $z$ is written $\Phi(z)$.
$P(Z < 1.2)$ is by definition the area under the curve to the left of $z = 1.2$, and that area is exactly what $\Phi$ records.
Because $1.2$ lies to the right of the centre $0$, more than half of the area is to its left, so we expect an answer comfortably above $0.5$.

steps.description: Read the value from the calculator or tables
steps.explanation: Using the normal cumulative function (or a standard normal table) gives the area to the left of $1.2$ directly.
This means about $88\%$ of the standard normal distribution lies below $z = 1.2$, which agrees with our expectation of a value above $0.5$.
Rounded to four significant figures this is the required probability.
